Name: distortion_rate_bounds
Title: Exploring the Fundamental Limits of Neural Network Compression: A Rate-Distortion Perspective
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental limits of neural network compression can be characterized by a rate-distortion curve, which trades off the number of bits required to represent the network against the resulting distortion in task performance. By leveraging information-theoretic tools and techniques, we can establish achievable bounds on the rate-distortion curve and develop practical compression algorithms that approach these limits.
Related Work: Previous work has explored the use of risk measures to design quantizers with uncertain distortion functions (Egan, 2025) and developed neural audio codecs that learn disentangled representations of speech attributes (Qian et al., 2026). However, these works do not provide a comprehensive understanding of the fundamental limits of neural network compression. Our proposal builds upon these efforts by establishing a rate-distortion framework for neural network compression and exploring the trade-offs between bits, distortion, and task performance.
Abstract: This proposal explores the fundamental limits of neural network compression from a rate-distortion perspective. We establish a framework for characterizing the trade-offs between the number of bits required to represent a neural network and the resulting distortion in task performance. By leveraging information-theoretic tools and techniques, we derive achievable bounds on the rate-distortion curve and develop practical compression algorithms that approach these limits. Our approach provides a comprehensive understanding of the limits of neural network compression and has implications for the development of efficient and effective compression algorithms.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments to validate our rate-distortion framework and evaluate the performance of our compression algorithms. Specifically, we will: (1) establish a baseline rate-distortion curve for a range of neural network architectures and tasks; (2) develop and evaluate the performance of practical compression algorithms that approach the achievable bounds; and (3) explore the trade-offs between bits, distortion, and task performance using our rate-distortion framework. We will use mutual information estimators and Fisher information sensitivities to allocate bits where they reduce task-relevant distortion most.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The main risk factors and limitations of our proposal are: (1) the development of accurate models for the distortion function, which may be uncertain or intractable to compute; (2) the complexity of the compression algorithms, which may be computationally expensive to implement; and (3) the potential for overfitting or underfitting in the compression process, which may affect the resulting distortion in task performance.
